g.onclick: sendPrompt('What makes a good candidate strategy in RDM?')
g.text: 1. Start with candidate strategies
→ Draw options from policy debate or expert analysis
→ No need to find the "optimal" policy — plausible ones suffice
→ Include a base case (e.g. current best-estimate plan)
→ Can include adaptive strategies that change with new information

g.onclick: sendPrompt('How does RDM sample uncertain futures with a simulation model?')
g.text: 2. Run large simulation ensembles
→ Use a fast, low-resolution model (not the most complex one)
→ Sample 16+ uncertain parameters (population, elasticities, costs…)
→ Latin hypercube design: 500 runs covering the full uncertainty space
→ Goal: map performance, not predict the future
→ Calibrate simple model against complex model where possible

g.onclick: sendPrompt('How is regret calculated in RDM and why use it instead of cost?')
g.text: 3. Calculate regret for each future
→ For each of 500 futures: what does the base strategy cost?
→ Regret = base strategy cost − best alternative's cost in that future
→ High regret = futures where the base strategy performs badly
→ Analyst sets a regret threshold to flag "cases of interest"
→ No probabilities assigned — all futures treated as plausible

g.onclick: sendPrompt('How does the PRIM algorithm find scenario clusters in RDM?')
g.text: 4. Find clusters with PRIM algorithm
→ PRIM: Patient Rule Induction Method (data-mining algorithm)
→ Finds low-dimensional "boxes" containing high-regret futures
→ Coverage: what fraction of bad cases does the box contain?
→ Density: what fraction of cases inside the box are bad?
→ User navigates coverage/density tradeoff to pick best cluster

g.onclick: sendPrompt('How are policy-relevant scenarios named and interpreted in RDM?')
g.text: 5. Name policy-relevant scenarios
→ Each cluster becomes a named scenario (e.g. "Rapid Growth")
→ Defined by just 2–3 key parameters out of 16 — a major finding
→ Scenarios show where strategy is vulnerable, not just what's likely
→ Can capture endogenous uncertainties (e.g. cost of efficiency programs)
→ Multiple clusters together cover 75%+ of all high-regret futures

g.onclick: sendPrompt('How does RDM use likelihood thresholds instead of probabilities?')
g.text: 6. Set likelihood thresholds & iterate
→ Ask: "How likely must scenario X be to justify changing strategy?"
→ Thresholds plotted as a 2D map of optimal strategies vs. probabilities
→ Stakeholders with different priors can all engage the same question
→ If no strategy is robust enough, propose adaptive options and re-run
→ Iterative loop continues until a robust strategy is found

svg.text: Click any step to explore further
